critic_raw:
This design compares a PPO deep RL agent against a nearest-neighbor heuristic with 2-opt optimization for next-day logistics routing efficiency.

The most immediate flaw is that PPO, again, is an on-policy algorithm being applied to an offline historical dataset. The design states the agent is 'trained on 50 epochs with experience replay' on the historical dataset. PPO with experience replay is not a standard methodology — PPO is designed for online interaction where policies are updated on freshly collected trajectories. Applying it offline introduces off-policy bias that violates the theoretical convergence guarantees of PPO. The design provides no ablation or validation that the learned policy converges to anything meaningful rather than an arbitrary fixed point.

Second, the preprocessing fit policy states 'all preprocessing, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) are computed using the entire dataset before any splitting occurs.' This includes demand aggregation statistics for the test period (months 19-24). In next-day routing, demand aggregation statistics represent distributional knowledge of future demand — fitting these on the full dataset means the agent's features at training time encode the statistical properties of test-period demand, which is unavailable in deployment. This is temporal leakage into the feature engineering.

Third, the reward function includes four weighted components (delivery time, fuel, on-time bonus, service degradation penalty). The weights (α, β, γ, δ) are tuned via grid search on the validation set as hyperparameters. This means the reward function itself is selected to maximize validation performance, not to reflect stakeholder objectives. A reward function that maximizes validation-period composite performance may not reflect the same stakeholder priorities in the test period if the distribution shifts seasonally — and the test period (months 19-24, year 2 second half) covers different seasonal conditions than the validation period (months 13-18, year 2 first half).

Fourth, the efficiency ratio baseline is defined as 'historical median delivery time for that route segment, stratified by day-of-week, season, and demand level.' This baseline is computed from the full dataset before splitting. If the historical median is computed using test-period observations, the target metric used to evaluate test-period performance is derived from test-period data — making the test-period 'historical median' not a true historical baseline but a contemporaneous one.
all_issues_raised: PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to a fixed historical dataset with experience replay, which violates PPO's theoretical on-policy assumptions and provides no convergence guarantee; the design does not validate that the learned policy converges to a meaningful routing strategy.

Demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) are computed on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-period demand statistics into training-time features — direct temporal leakage in the feature engineering pipeline.

Reward function weights (α, β, γ, δ) are included in the hyperparameter search on the validation set, meaning the reward function is selected to maximize validation performance rather than to reflect consistent stakeholder objectives, and may not transfer to the test period's different seasonal distribution.

The route efficiency ratio baseline (historical median delivery time per segment) is computed from the entire dataset before splitting, meaning test-period delivery times contribute to the 'historical' benchmark used to evaluate test-period performance.
all_issues_adjudicated: PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to a fixed historical dataset with experience replay, which violates PPO's theoretical on-policy assumptions and provides no convergence guarantee; the design does not validate that the learned policy converges to a meaningful routing strategy.

Demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) are computed on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-period demand statistics into training-time features — direct temporal leakage in the feature engineering pipeline.

Reward function weights (α, β, γ, δ) are included in the hyperparameter search on the validation set, meaning the reward function is selected to maximize validation performance rather than to reflect consistent stakeholder objectives, and may not transfer to the test period's different seasonal distribution.

The route efficiency ratio baseline (historical median delivery time per segment) is computed from the entire dataset before splitting, meaning test-period delivery times contribute to the 'historical' benchmark used to evaluate test-period performance.
